candidates.name: Sunshine Loans Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 24-074MR Court finds Sunshine Loans charged customers prohibited fees
SunshineLoans Pty Ltd v Australian Securities and Investments Commission [2026] HCA 8
25-046MR Full Federal Court finds in favour of ASIC in two appeals concerning Sunshine Loans
26-052MR High Court finds in favour of ASIC on an appeal by Sunshine Loans

candidates.public_interest_reason: Federal Court found Sunshine Loans Pty Ltd contravened the National Credit Code and National Consumer Credit Protection Act 2009 by charging a prohibited $35 'amendment fee' on over 670,000 small amount credit contracts between 1 July 2016 and 2 November 2020, in proceedings brought by ASIC seeking $10.5 million in penalties. The company's application to have the trial judge recuse himself from the penalty hearing (on apprehended-bias grounds) was dismissed by the High Court of Australia on 18 March 2026 ([2026] HCA 8). Following the trial judge's recusal from the penalty phase (on apprehended-bias grounds relating to his credibility findings against Sunshine Loans and its witnesses), ASIC successfully appealed that recusal decision to the Full Federal Court (March 2025) and then, after Sunshine Loans' further appeal, to the High Court, which found unanimously (7-0) in ASIC's favour on 18 March 2026. The penalty hearing has now been remitted to the original trial judge, Justice Roger Derrington, for determination.
risk_indicators: Found by the Federal Court to have contravened the National Credit Code/NCCP Act by charging a prohibited fee on over 670,000 small amount credit contracts

candidates.name: United Super Pty Ltd (trustee for Cbus)

source_evidence.title: APRA accepts court enforceable undertaking from Cbus and launches investigation

candidates.name: Reebelo Australia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Reebelo Australia pays penalties for alleged false or misleading statements about consumer guarantee rights

candidates.public_interest_reason: Reebelo Australia, an online marketplace for new and refurbished electronics, paid $59,400 in penalties after the ACCC issued three infringement notices alleging false or misleading statements about consumer guarantee rights. The statements purported to impose a 14-day time limit on consumers' ability to obtain a remedy for faulty, damaged, misdescribed or wrong-model goods, when the Australian Consumer Law entitles consumers to a remedy regardless of elapsed time. The ACCC noted particular concern given the products involved are often refurbished high-end electronics such as laptops and mobile phones.
risk_indicators: Paid $59,400 in penalties across three ACCC infringement notices for alleged false or misleading consumer-guarantee representations

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC opposed Yamaha Motor Australia Pty Ltd's proposed acquisition of Telwater Pty Ltd (announced 18 December 2025) following an in-depth investigation, finding it likely to substantially lessen competition in the wholesale supply of outboard motors in Australia. candidates.name: Telstra Group Limited

source_evidence.title: Telstra to pay $50m penalty for unconscionable sales to Indigenous consumers
Telstra fined $18 million for misleading Belong customers over broadband speed claims

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Telstra to pay $50 million — the second-highest penalty ever imposed under the Australian Consumer Law — for unconscionable conduct after sales staff at five licensed Telstra-branded stores in the Northern Territory, South Australia and Western Australia signed up 108 Indigenous consumers to multiple post-paid mobile contracts between January 2016 and August 2018 that they did not understand and could not afford. The Court found staff manipulated credit assessments, misrepresented products as free, and exploited customers' social, language, literacy and cultural vulnerabilities, leaving them with debts to Telstra ranging from $1,600 to almost $20,000. Telstra also gave the ACCC an enforceable undertaking to remediate affected consumers, improve its compliance program and expand its Indigenous telephone hotline and digital literacy program. In a separate ACCC matter, the Federal Court found Telstra made false or misleading representations after migrating 8,897 Belong NBN customers, in October-November 2020, from a plan with a 40 Mbps maximum upload speed to one with only 20 Mbps, without notifying them, in breach of the Australian Consumer Law. Telstra was fined $18 million, with remediation to affected customers exceeding $2.3 million.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $50 million, the second-highest ever under the Australian Consumer Law

candidates.public_interest_reason: WorkSafe WA commenced prosecution against Greenmount Resources Pty Ltd, as mine operator, over the death of a contractor's employee at the Karlawinda mine in October 2022, when a dump truck collided with the utility vehicle he was in, alleging failure to ensure worker health and safety resulting in death under the Work Health and Safety Act 2020. The maximum penalty is $3.5 million.
risk_indicators: Subject of an ongoing WorkSafe WA prosecution alleging a health and safety failure resulting in a worker's death
Maximum penalty of $3.5 million

candidates.name: Greenova Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Court fines solar installer $9,000 over unsafe battery installs

candidates.public_interest_reason: Greenova Pty Ltd pleaded guilty in the Seymour Magistrates' Court to three charges under Victoria's Electricity Safety Act 1998 over non-compliant battery energy storage system installations at five Victorian properties between December 2023 and July 2024, including a property at Toolamba where an incorrectly installed battery overcharged and ignited, prompting a Country Fire Authority callout. The company was fined $9,000.
risk_indicators: Pleaded guilty to three electrical safety offences over non-compliant battery installations
One installation caused a battery to overcharge and ignite, requiring a fire brigade callout
Fined $9,000 by the court

candidates.name: Peak Gold Mines Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Peak Gold Mines Pty Ltd convicted and fined following death of mine worker

candidates.public_interest_reason: Peak Gold Mines Pty Ltd was convicted and fined $480,000 plus costs by the NSW District Court (21 October 2021) for breaching its duty under the Work Health and Safety Act 2011, following the death of worker Mark Brilley on 3 April 2017 near Cobar. Brilley died from heat and diesel exhaust exposure while operating an open-cabin drill rig approximately one kilometre underground after a ventilation fan had been shut down for maintenance.
risk_indicators: Convicted of a work health and safety offence following a worker's death
Fined $480,000 plus costs by the NSW District Court

candidates.name: Grocery Delivery E-Services Australia Pty Ltd (trading as HelloFresh)

source_evidence.title: HelloFresh and Youfoodz in court over alleged subscription traps

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC commenced Federal Court proceedings (around 16 December 2025) alleging Grocery Delivery E-Services Australia Pty Ltd, trading as HelloFresh, breached the Australian Consumer Law over subscription cancellation traps: 62,061 customers were allegedly charged between January 2023 and March 2025 despite cancelling before the advertised cut-off. The ACCC also alleges HelloFresh required payment details to view its menu while representing that consumers would not be charged unless meals were selected.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court proceedings brought by the ACCC alleging misleading subscription cancellation representations, ongoing
Alleged that over 62,000 customers were charged despite cancelling before the advertised deadline

candidates.name: Youfoodz Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: HelloFresh and Youfoodz in court over alleged subscription traps

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC commenced separate Federal Court proceedings (around 16 December 2025) alleging Youfoodz Pty Ltd, also owned by HelloFresh SE, breached the Australian Consumer Law over subscription cancellation traps: 39,408 customers were allegedly charged between October 2022 and November 2024 despite cancelling before the advertised cut-off.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court proceedings brought by the ACCC alleging misleading subscription cancellation representations, ongoing
Alleged that over 39,000 customers were charged despite cancelling before the advertised deadline

candidates.name: Sika Australia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Western Sydney company fined $30,000 for dangerous chemicals storage

candidates.public_interest_reason: The NSW Environment Protection Authority fined Sika Australia $30,000 (7 February 2025) after an inspection of its Wetherill Park facility found flammable chemicals stockpiled and stored outside, cement spilled next to a stormwater drain, and a damaged reactor vessel leaking chemicals in a zone classified as having an explosive gas atmosphere. The EPA also issued a Clean Up Notice requiring the reactor to be taken out of use until repaired and site chemical-storage practices improved.
risk_indicators: Fined $30,000 by the NSW EPA over dangerous chemical storage practices
Regulator found a damaged reactor vessel leaking chemicals in an explosive-gas-atmosphere zone
Issued a Clean Up Notice requiring remediation before the reactor could be used again

candidates.name: 3M Company and 3M Australia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Legal proceedings against 3M Company and 3M Australia Pty Ltd

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Commonwealth commenced Federal Court proceedings on 28 May 2026 against 3M Company and 3M Australia Pty Ltd, seeking more than $2 billion in damages over PFAS ('forever chemicals') contamination arising from aqueous film-forming foam used at 28 Defence bases. The Commonwealth alleges 3M withheld information and misrepresented the environmental risks of its firefighting foam, and gave assurances about its disposal and environmental safety inconsistent with its own internal knowledge.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court proceedings brought by the Commonwealth seeking more than $2 billion in damages, ongoing
Alleged to have withheld information and misrepresented environmental risks of a product used at 28 Defence bases
Allegations concern PFAS contamination, a substance associated with long-term environmental and health effects

candidates.name: Electro Optic Systems Holdings Limited

source_evidence.title: 26-068MR Electro Optic Systems Holdings ordered to pay $4 million penalty for continuous disclosure breaches

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court found that defence and space systems manufacturer Electro Optic Systems Holdings Limited breached its continuous disclosure obligations under the Corporations Act. EOS knew by 25 July 2022 that its 2022 revenue would be approximately $164 million, well below its stated guidance of at least $212.3 million, but did not correct the market until 31 October 2022, a delay of roughly 14 weeks. The company was ordered to pay a $4 million penalty plus ASIC's costs.
risk_indicators: Found by the Federal Court to have breached continuous disclosure obligations to the market
Alleged to have known its revenue would fall well short of stated guidance for roughly 14 weeks before correcting the market
Ordered to pay a $4 million penalty plus costs

candidates.name: Regional Express Holdings Limited

source_evidence.title: 26-140MR Rex held accountable for continuous disclosure failure, three non-executive directors did not breach duties

candidates.public_interest_reason: The NSW Supreme Court found Regional Express Holdings Limited (Rex) breached its continuous disclosure obligations over a 28 February 2023 profit forecast, having lacked reasonable grounds from 14 April 2023 to expect a positive FY23 operating profit before disclosing a $35 million operational loss forecast on 20 June 2023. Former executive chair Lim Kim Hai admitted contraventions and accepted a pecuniary penalty and disqualification; ASIC's case against three non-executive directors was unsuccessful and they were cleared.
risk_indicators: Found by the NSW Supreme Court to have breached continuous disclosure obligations over a profit forecast
Former executive chair admitted contraventions and accepted a pecuniary penalty and disqualification
Three non-executive directors were separately found not to have breached their duties

candidates.name: Mobil Oil Australia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Mobil to pay $16m in penalties for misleading statements about fuel sold at nine petrol stations

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Mobil Oil Australia Pty Ltd to pay $16 million in penalties (17 February 2026) for false or misleading representations at nine Queensland petrol stations, branding fuel as 'Mobil Synergy Fuel' with additives between August 2020 and July 2024 when it was substantially the same as unadditised fuel sold elsewhere. Mobil was also required to publish corrective notices and implement an Australian Consumer Law compliance program.
risk_indicators: Ordered by the Federal Court to pay a $16 million penalty for misleading fuel-quality representations
Required to publish corrective notices and implement a consumer law compliance program

candidates.name: WiseTech Global Limited

source_evidence.title: WiseTech to divest Expedient following ACCC investigation

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC accepted a court-enforceable undertaking (accepted 30 December 2025, announced 5 January 2026) requiring WiseTech Global Limited and its subsidiary BluJay Solutions (Australia) Pty Ltd to divest Expedient, a logistics software business acquired as part of WiseTech's e2open acquisition completed in August 2025, to an ACCC-approved buyer capable of operating it as a viable competitor to WiseTech's CargoWise platform. The ACCC found WiseTech completed the acquisition before its competition review had concluded despite being aware the deal raised significant competition concerns - a 'gun-jumping' issue under Australia's new mandatory pre-completion merger notification regime, which took effect 1 January 2026.
risk_indicators: Subject to a court-enforceable undertaking requiring divestiture of an acquired business after an ACCC competition investigation
Regulator found the underlying acquisition was completed before its competition review concluded, despite awareness of competition concerns
Matter arises under Australia's new mandatory pre-completion merger notification regime

candidates.name: Transitional Funding Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 26-001MR ASIC suspends Australian credit licence of Transitional Funding Pty Ltd

candidates.public_interest_reason: ASIC suspended the Australian credit licence of Transitional Funding Pty Ltd, effective 19 December 2025, for failing to comply with its licence's 'Key Person' condition (requiring notification to ASIC within five business days of a key person ceasing duties, and lodging a variation naming a replacement) and for failing to pay industry funding levies owed to ASIC. The suspension was subsequently extended by ASIC on 16 June 2026 to 17 December 2026.
risk_indicators: Australian credit licence suspended by ASIC, and the suspension later extended
Found to have failed to comply with a key-person notification licence condition
Found to have failed to pay industry funding levies owed to ASIC

candidates.name: Centre Capital Securities Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 26-020MR ASIC cancels AFS and AC licences of Centre Capital Securities for failure to pay industry funding levies

candidates.public_interest_reason: ASIC cancelled both the Australian Financial Services licence and Australian Credit licence of Centre Capital Securities Pty Ltd, effective 29 January 2026, after the company failed to pay industry funding levies owed to ASIC for more than 12 months, covering the 2017-18 to 2022-23 financial years.
risk_indicators: Both its AFS licence and Australian Credit licence cancelled by ASIC
Found to have failed to pay industry funding levies owed to ASIC across six financial years

candidates.name: Ausfinancial Pty Ltd (trading as Swoosh Finance)

source_evidence.title: 24-285MR ASIC takes action against Swoosh alleging responsible lending failures and DDO breaches

candidates.public_interest_reason: ASIC commenced Federal Court civil penalty proceedings alleging Ausfinancial Pty Ltd, trading as Swoosh Finance, breached responsible lending obligations for at least 11 consumers on medium-amount credit contracts of $2,000 to $5,000 secured against vehicles, boats and trailers, by failing to properly inquire into and verify those borrowers' financial situations. ASIC further alleges Swoosh breached design and distribution obligations by failing to review its target market determinations despite mounting consumer complaints. The proceedings remain unresolved, with trial listed for March 2027.
risk_indicators: Subject of ongoing Federal Court civil penalty proceedings brought by ASIC
Alleged to have failed to properly verify borrowers' financial situations before extending credit
Alleged to have failed to review target market determinations despite rising consumer complaints

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Aged Care Quality and Safety Commission entered an Enforceable Undertaking with Infinite Aged Care (Cornubia) Pty Ltd as part of a sector-wide enforcement round (announced 13 January 2025) against 11 providers found to be falling well short of mandatory care minutes targets for residents over successive quarters.

Named among 11 providers found to be falling well short of mandatory resident care-minutes targets

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Aged Care Quality and Safety Commission entered an Enforceable Undertaking with Havilah Hostel Inc, a Maryborough, Victoria facility holding an overall 3-star rating as at 17 July 2025, signed 3 October 2025. candidates.name: Better Together Support Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: NDIS Quality and Safeguards Commission compliance and enforcement actions search (notice of revocation of registration, Better Together Support Pty Ltd, 30 March 2026)

candidates.public_interest_reason: The NDIS Quality and Safeguards Commission issued a notice of revocation of registration to Better Together Support Pty Ltd (a registered provider of group and centre-based activities in Peakhurst, NSW) on 30 March 2026, on the basis a delegate of the Commissioner reasonably believed the company contravened section 73J of the NDIS Act by failing to complete an assessment of the remaining elements of its Certification audit due three months after service delivery commenced. The revocation took effect from 5pm on 8 June 2026.
risk_indicators: NDIS Commission notice of revocation of registration issued over an alleged contravention of section 73J of the NDIS Act

candidates.name: Reinforced Concrete Pipes Australia (NSW) Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: EPA secures Enforceable Undertaking with concrete company following investigation

candidates.public_interest_reason: The NSW EPA agreed to a $100,000 Enforceable Undertaking with Reinforced Concrete Pipes Australia (NSW) Pty Ltd following an investigation that began when the company applied for an Environment Protection Licence in December 2022 and the EPA found it had been producing more than 30,000 tonnes of steel-reinforced concrete pipes annually at its Somersby facility between 2018 and 2022 without any licence at all — operating for several years with no formal monitoring of dust, water pollution or noise. The EU payment funds local environmental restoration projects, including platypus population monitoring and protection.
risk_indicators: NSW EPA enforceable undertaking of $100,000 following an unlicensed-operation investigation
Company operated a concrete manufacturing facility producing over 30,000 tonnes annually for at least four years without any Environment Protection Licence
No formal monitoring of dust, water pollution or noise impacts during the unlicensed operating period

candidates.name: Dusk Australasia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: ACCC takes Dusk to Federal Court over alleged button battery safety breaches

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC commenced Federal Court proceedings on 22 July 2026 against homewares retailer Dusk Australasia Pty Ltd (a wholly-owned subsidiary of ASX-listed Dusk Group Limited), alleging it supplied 66,453 products across 29 product lines (candles, diffusers, tealights, snow globes and other LED items) containing button batteries between 17 May 2023 and 12 December 2024 in breach of mandatory safety and information standards under the Australian Consumer Law. The ACCC also alleges Dusk breached a court-enforceable undertaking it had already given in April 2023, after an earlier matter in which Dusk paid $106,560 across eight infringement notices for non-compliant button-battery Halloween products.
risk_indicators: Subject of ACCC Federal Court proceedings alleging breach of mandatory product safety and information standards
Alleged breach of a pre-existing court-enforceable undertaking from an earlier, related 2023 enforcement matter
Matter concerns tens of thousands of consumer products containing button batteries, a hazard the ACCC treats as a standing national child-safety priority

candidates.name: A. Haros Demolition Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Demolition company fined $72,000 after worker suffers amputation

candidates.public_interest_reason: A. Haros Demolition Pty Ltd was convicted and fined $72,000 (reduced from $120,000 for an early guilty plea) by the South Australian Employment Court on 2 July 2026 following a SafeWork SA prosecution for a Category 2 offence under the Work Health and Safety Act 2012. A SafeWork SA investigation found the company failed to maintain an exclusion zone around an operating excavator, failed to implement an effective communication system between workers and the operator, and had no spotter present, at a Thebarton demolition site on 15 November 2023 - a worker was struck in the foot by the excavator bucket and suffered a crush injury resulting in amputation of all toes and part of the forefoot.
risk_indicators: Convicted of a Category 2 offence under the Work Health and Safety Act 2012 following a SafeWork SA prosecution
Court findings included absent exclusion zone, absent spotter, and no effective operator-worker communication system around heavy machinery
Incident resulted in a worker's partial foot amputation
